children.name: Oceanites
children.description: Small, sooty-brown storm petrels with a conspicuous white rump and pale underwing bar. They patter on the water surface with a distinctive fluttering flight, feeding on plankton and small fish. The genus includes Wilson's Storm Petrel, one of the most abundant and widely distributed seabirds on Earth.

children.name: Oceanites oceanicus
children.description: One of the most abundant birds on Earth, breeding in huge colonies on Antarctic coasts and islands. It migrates to northern oceans during the southern winter, with some individuals reaching the Arctic Circle. It feeds by skittering across the water surface, pattering its feet to stir up plankton.

children.name: Oceanites pincanus
children.description: A recently described storm petrel from the coastal waters of Chile, named after the Pincoya, a mythical sea spirit from Chiloé folklore. It is similar to Wilson's Storm Petrel but has a longer tail and different vocalisations. Its breeding grounds were only discovered in 2021 in the coastal mountains of southern Chile.

children.name: Garrodia
children.description: A monotypic genus of small storm petrel with a distinctive grey back and dark cap, giving it a hooded appearance. It breeds exclusively on islands in the Southern Ocean.

children.name: Garrodia nereis
children.description: A small storm petrel with a pale grey back, white underparts, and a dark cap that contrasts strongly with the white face. It breeds on sub-Antarctic islands including the Falklands, South Georgia, and the Antipodes. It feeds on plankton and small crustaceans, often foraging far from its breeding colonies.

children.name: Pelagodroma
children.description: A monotypic genus of storm petrel with extraordinarily long legs that trail well beyond the tail in flight. The White-faced Storm Petrel has a distinctive appearance with a white face and dark eyestripe, resembling a tiny shearwater.

children.name: Pelagodroma marina
children.description: A distinctive storm petrel with a white face and forehead, dark eyestripe, and pale grey upperparts. It breeds on islands in the South Atlantic and South Pacific, with outlying colonies in the Canary Islands and Cape Verde. It feeds by pattering on the surface and picking prey from the water, its long legs trailing behind.

children.name: Fregetta
children.description: Storm petrels with a distinctive white belly variably streaked with dark, contrasting with a sooty-black head and upperparts. They breed on remote islands in the Southern Ocean and tropical Atlantic, foraging widely in pelagic waters.

children.name: Fregetta tropica
children.description: A storm petrel of the Southern Ocean with a black head and upperparts, white underparts, and a diagnostic narrow black line running down the centre of the belly. It breeds on sub-Antarctic islands and migrates north of the Antarctic Convergence in winter. Like other storm petrels, it patter-feeds on surface plankton.

children.name: Fregetta maoriana
children.description: A small storm petrel thought extinct for over 150 years until rediscovered in 2003 in the Hauraki Gulf of New Zealand. It has a dark head and upperparts, white underparts with a variable dark belly stripe, and a white rump. It breeds on islands off the coast of New Zealand and is classified as Critically Endangered.

children.name: Fregetta grallaria
children.description: A storm petrel with a black head and upperparts, a white belly with no dark central stripe, and white underwing linings. It breeds on remote islands in the Australasian region including Lord Howe Island and the Kermadec Islands. It forages widely in tropical and subtropical waters, feeding on surface plankton.

children.name: Fregetta lineata
children.description: A little-known storm petrel described from New Caledonia, with distinctive dark streaking on the white underparts. It is known from very few specimens and sightings and its breeding grounds remain unknown. It may be one of the rarest seabirds in the world.

children.name: Nesofregetta
children.description: A monotypic genus of large, strikingly patterned storm petrel found in the tropical Pacific Ocean. It is the largest storm petrel in the Southern Ocean and has a distinctive white collar and breast band.

children.name: Nesofregetta fuliginosa
children.description: A large, striking storm petrel with a white collar, white belly, dark breast band, and greyish upperparts, found in the tropical Pacific Ocean. It breeds on remote islands from the Marshall Islands to the Marquesas and Tuamotu archipelagos. It is classified as Vulnerable due to predation by introduced species on its breeding islands.
